mnemonics.title: Format Specifier Types
mnemonics.content: DID F CX SO UHP: Decimal (d), Integer (i), Float (f), Character (c), String (s), Octal (o), Unsigned (u), Hex (x), Pointer (p)

mnemonics.title: Precision Meaning
mnemonics.content: For f: decimals shown. For s: chars printed. For d: minimum digits (pad with zeros).

mnemonics.title: Field Width Rule
mnemonics.content: Positive width = right align + space pad. Negative width = left align + space pad. Leading zero in width = zero pad (except for floats).

acronyms.usage: The name indicates it prints data in a specified format to standard output
Header file containing printf, scanf, and other I/O functions

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a 5-character box: %5d with 42 becomes | 42| (3 spaces + 2 digits = 5 characters).
%.2f is like rounding to 2 decimal places: 3.14159 → 3.14 (cut off after 2 decimals)
\n = new line (down+left), \t = tab (jump to next column), \r = carriage return (back to start of line)

analogies.analogy: Think of format string as a sentence template with blanks: "My name is ___ and I am ___ years old" with %s and %d filling the blanks.
Field width is like a fixed-size parking space. If your car (value) is smaller, it gets padded with spaces (empty space) to fill the lot.
Precision for %s acts like a character limit on social media - %.5s only shows first 5 characters of a longer string.
